name: Detect T1072: Software Deployment Tools Misuse on Windows

action--2153426d-aff3-43cf-ae48-720ee29b.name: Use SIEM or EDR tools to alert on deviations from the baseline, such as unusual execution chains or timing (e.g., executions outside of maintenance windows).

commands.command: Use SIEM or EDR tools to alert on deviations from the baseline, such as unusual execution chains or timing (e.g., executions outside of maintenance windows).

action--2465dab0-0c96-4937-beee-48773f1c.name: Monitor for processes spawned by Intune management components, such as the Intune Management Extension agent, when they initiate scripts or binaries.

commands.command: Monitor for processes spawned by Intune management components, such as the Intune Management Extension agent, when they initiate scripts or binaries.
commands.description: Monitor for processes spawned by Intune management components, such as the Intune Management Extension agent, when they initiate scripts or binaries.

action--3100191b-e2e0-4684-a9ed-3821b58b.name: Review and investigate alerts for false positives, ensuring legitimate administrative actions are whitelisted or tuned out.

commands.command: Review and investigate alerts for false positives, ensuring legitimate administrative actions are whitelisted or tuned out.
commands.description: Review and investigate alerts for false positives, ensuring legitimate administrative actions are whitelisted or tuned out.

action--57f73316-7719-4e30-813e-f0d3e666.name: Identify and monitor processes spawned by SCCM (System Center Configuration Manager) components, such as CcmExec.exe or Smsexec.exe, especially when they launch scripting engines like PowerShell or cmd.exe.

commands.command: Identify and monitor processes spawned by SCCM (System Center Configuration Manager) components, such as CcmExec.exe or Smsexec.exe, especially when they launch scripting engines like PowerShell or cmd.exe.
commands.description: Identify and monitor processes spawned by SCCM (System Center Configuration Manager) components, such as CcmExec.exe or Smsexec.exe, especially when they launch scripting engines like PowerShell or cmd.exe.

action--721336d7-0bf1-403d-befd-8ba08cbf.name: Detect execution of scripts or binaries from unusual console applications, such as cmtrace.exe (a log viewer tool) launching PowerShell or cmd.exe, which may indicate abuse.

commands.command: Detect execution of scripts or binaries from unusual console applications, such as cmtrace.exe (a log viewer tool) launching PowerShell or cmd.exe, which may indicate abuse.
commands.description: Detect execution of scripts or binaries from unusual console applications, such as cmtrace.exe (a log viewer tool) launching PowerShell or cmd.exe, which may indicate abuse.

action--a688d8c7-5697-4545-b654-d32d4bb5.name: Filter events where the parent process is a known management tool (e.g., CcmExec.exe, Smsexec.exe, IntuneManagementExtension.exe, cmtrace.exe).

commands.command: Filter events where the parent process is a known management tool (e.g., CcmExec.exe, Smsexec.exe, IntuneManagementExtension.exe, cmtrace.exe).

action--abc85807-05bb-4421-a67c-66933e34.name: If malicious activity is confirmed, isolate affected systems, terminate malicious processes, and initiate incident response procedures.

commands.command: If malicious activity is confirmed, isolate affected systems, terminate malicious processes, and initiate incident response procedures.
commands.description: If malicious activity is confirmed, isolate affected systems, terminate malicious processes, and initiate incident response procedures.

action--ca1c6d4b-ae15-4335-9551-7db80950.name: Correlate with network connections to detect if spawned processes initiate connections to suspicious or external IP addresses.

commands.command: Correlate with network connections to detect if spawned processes initiate connections to suspicious or external IP addresses.

action--d637ec28-e872-42f3-9604-bd9ff381.name: Correlate with user context; note instances where SYSTEM or a service account is the parent process user, as this is common for legitimate management but may be abused.

commands.command: Correlate with user context; note instances where SYSTEM or a service account is the parent process user, as this is common for legitimate management but may be abused.

action--db51e7bc-83b4-4c4f-a853-126816bf.name: Enable and collect Windows Event Logs, specifically Event ID 4688 (process creation) with command line logging enabled, or Sysmon Event ID 1.

commands.command: Enable and collect Windows Event Logs, specifically Event ID 4688 (process creation) with command line logging enabled, or Sysmon Event ID 1.

action--fad84b9b-45e3-41d6-8499-8dd4719c.name: Establish a baseline of normal software deployment and management activities for SCCM and Intune in your environment.

commands.command: Establish a baseline of normal software deployment and management activities for SCCM and Intune in your environment.

action--fd4c4bed-be44-45b9-a8fc-90c0a16b.name: Look for child processes that are scripting engines (e.g., powershell.exe, cmd.exe, wscript.exe) or other binaries being deployed or executed.

commands.command: Look for child processes that are scripting engines (e.g., powershell.exe, cmd.exe, wscript.exe) or other binaries being deployed or executed.

parallel--8ef42baa-8d75-487e-930b-a9f1fb.name: Collect and analyze process creation events with a focus on parent-child relationships and user context

description: This is an automatically generated playbook based on the question: A detections playbook for T1072 of MITRE ATT&CK Matrix for Enterprise framework is required.The target platform is Windows. You can find more information at https://attack.mitre.org/detectionstrategies/DET0223#AN0623 . The description of the approach follows: Detects SCCM, Intune, or remote push execution spawning scripts or binaries from SYSTEM context or unusual consoles (e.g., cmtrace.exe launching PowerShell or cmd.exe).

external_references.name: MITRE ATT&CK T1072: Software Deployment Tools

items.content: Identify and monitor processes spawned by SCCM (System Center Configuration Manager) components, such as CcmExec.exe or Smsexec.exe, especially when they launch scripting engines like PowerShell or cmd.exe.
Monitor for processes spawned by Intune management components, such as the Intune Management Extension agent, when they initiate scripts or binaries.
Detect execution of scripts or binaries from unusual console applications, such as cmtrace.exe (a log viewer tool) launching PowerShell or cmd.exe, which may indicate abuse.
Enable and collect Windows Event Logs, specifically Event ID 4688 (process creation) with command line logging enabled, or Sysmon Event ID 1.
Filter events where the parent process is a known management tool (e.g., CcmExec.exe, Smsexec.exe, IntuneManagementExtension.exe, cmtrace.exe).
Look for child processes that are scripting engines (e.g., powershell.exe, cmd.exe, wscript.exe) or other binaries being deployed or executed.
Correlate with user context; note instances where SYSTEM or a service account is the parent process user, as this is common for legitimate management but may be abused.
Collect and analyze process creation events with a focus on parent-child relationships and user context
Establish a baseline of normal software deployment and management activities for SCCM and Intune in your environment.
Use SIEM or EDR tools to alert on deviations from the baseline, such as unusual execution chains or timing (e.g., executions outside of maintenance windows).
Correlate with network connections to detect if spawned processes initiate connections to suspicious or external IP addresses.
Review and investigate alerts for false positives, ensuring legitimate administrative actions are whitelisted or tuned out.
If malicious activity is confirmed, isolate affected systems, terminate malicious processes, and initiate incident response procedures.
